title: Girl Dies After Expensive Gene-Editing Therapy
summary: A couple paid over $800,000 for an experimental AAV-based gene-editing therapy for their daughter&\#x27;s rare brain disorder, but she died from complications likely due to an immune reaction to the viral vector. This case underscores the severe risks of AAV-based gene therapies, especially when delivered directly to the brain, and raises critical ethical questions about informed consent and the commercialization of unproven treatments. The therapy used an AAV vector to deliver gene-editing components directly into the brain, despite known risks of immune reactions. Animal studies had shown similar side effects, but these were downplayed by the researchers.
context: Adeno-associated virus \(AAV\) is a common vector in gene therapy due to its ability to infect both dividing and non-dividing cells and its low pathogenicity. However, AAV vectors can trigger immune responses, including severe liver or brain inflammation, and several approved AAV therapies carry black box warnings for such reactions. Direct injection into the brain amplifies these risks because the immune system may attack the viral capsid. This case involved a never-before-tried gene-editing approach, making the risk assessment even more uncertain.
discussion: Commenters expressed shock that AAV was chosen for brain delivery given known immunogenicity in approved therapies. Several noted that the researchers downplayed risks, ignored similar side effects in monkeys, and advanced an experimental treatment with unclear benefits, raising serious ethical concerns.

references.title: AAV-Based Gene Therapy: Opportunities, Risks, and Scale-Up Strategies
